speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] The interviewer is Donna Puccini in Chicago, USA, in English. Margot, you were telling us that you had gone back to Tarnow, and you had gone to the house that your husband had lived in and hidden things?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not to the house where my husband had hidden things. I-- we went-- we were invited to stay with a friend of my husband's. And we stayed there. And then my husband had told the police-- the Polish police that he had something to take out from a Polish apartment, where he had hidden things. He will tell you about that. And so we-- he went there. I didn't go with him. And so they took it out with my-- my brother-in-law. He went with my brother-in-law, and he took it out. And so he started to, to sell a little bit. And that's the way we lived. And then, of course, we went to Krakau, we went to Krakau to see whether there was something. And then we went to France.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you In Tarnow?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Tarnow? I don't really remember how long we were in Tarnow. Not very long. We went then to Krakau. And then in Krakau, we wanted to get a visa to America. I was on the German quota. Yeah, I could have gone all the way to America. Because my aunt, who gave us a certificate already-- I had an affidavit already during the war. In the ghetto their was once a registration. The Gestapo was there, and we had to bring our papers. So I had my papers to go. Even-- in Germany, we all had affidavits. My brothers all went. And I had an affidavit, too. When I-- they took my affidavit, the Germans, but I-- then after the war, I could have gone right away to, to America. But my husband was the Polish quota, so I could not-- you know, go by myself. I didn't want to leave him again.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I stayed and waited. So we had to wait. And it was a tense transit, like we went to France. We had a-- in Krakau there was the French, the French consulate. And I went to the French consulate.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, I would like to go to France with my family. And he said, OK, I give you a visa. We had to pay for it. And we went to France. And we opened up a factory with one of the guys who was on the photo-- he was a manufacturer. And we were-- my husband already knew the line, so we opened up a shirt factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Not on a big scale, because in France you couldn't make it-- it was after the war-- you couldn't get the, the fabric. You had to pay under the table. It was [GERMAN], which means rationed. Everything was rationed. But we knew somebody who had a millinery that made their own fabric. So he gave us the material. And we opened up a factory. But in France, it was like you had-- it was a commercial apartment. So we had on one side, we had the apartment. It was on the second floor. And on the other side was the-- where we manufactured. We, we cut there-- we cut the merchandise there. And then we gave it out to sew. And then it came back, and we-- and then it was packed there. So that was the factory. But it was very nice because-- and then, of course, you know, we were afraid that the communists coming in-- de Gaulle or somebody, there, it was. So we, we, we left for, for America. And we got already the affidavits. We had to wait two years or so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit more about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we lived--

speaker: interviewer
text: --your life in France. Because--

speaker: interviewee
text: In France?

speaker: interviewer
text: You were there a long time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you, in France, they wanted to make us French citizen. And they came to our apartment. We lived there seven or eight years we were in France. And my children-- it was good at one time that we should be citizens. But my dream was to go to America to my brothers, anyhow. Because I had the two brothers. I had an Aunt, which was wonderful to us. She always sent little packages and things like that for the kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I wanted to go to America. So we didn't even bother with being French citizen. But we-- it was very good, because when I was pregnant, they paid for every child that was born after the war. They gave a certain amount of money-- a [FRENCH] it was called. So I got the rations like chocolate and, and-- which other people didn't get-- because I was pregnant three times there. So and when the kids were born, I got a little bit more food and things like that. But it was a [GERMAN] everything for us in the beginning.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I got very sick. I was in the hospital with my-- with my eye. And I was in the hospital because I was so under-nourished and all my vitamins deficiencies. And my whole body was-- it's still there, some things which are not OK, but listen.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your pregnancies like?

speaker: interviewee
text: My first pregnancy I was in bed the whole time. I started to bleed when I was five weeks pregnant. Till the end, I had to be in bed. And I had to go to a private clinic, because I was so terribly weak. And all the money we, we had, we gave for this little baby, my oldest daughter. And then the second daughter was already better. And then the third pregnancy was already better, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a difficult decision to begin a family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was a difficult-- as a matter of fact, I didn't even-- I wasn't so much to, to have a family. But my husband was very much for it. And of course, I wanted to have maybe one child, just to have something to, to show for that I'm a women, you know? But I was afraid that they will not be normal. And that was my terrible, terrible nightmares I had for each pregnancy. I saw that the kids will have inherited what I went through. The horror things, you know, the horror. That, that they will have, they will not be normal or something like this. In the meantime, it was just vice versa, thank God.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. Who was with you in France? In France? Only my husband and my brother-in-law at the time, you know. And my brother-in-law-- my husband will tell you about my brother-in-law.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a problem with him because he did not want to live after the war. And he lived with us for a while, and then he remarried. He wanted to have another family. And the wife he married, she told him somehow she was a little bit younger than she really was in real life. You know, people didn't have identification too much. So they never had a child. And he was-- he was just longing for somebody, again to have somebody. So he had nobody. And he didn't-- he did not want to exist. I mean, he didn't want to go to, to make a suit, you know, to a dressmaker or somebody who did something for him. He wouldn't want anything. But, so he lived for 15 years with another wife, without children. But his life was never a life. He didn't want to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said that you knew your brothers were in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: My--I had some contact. I had the brother in Israel. And I had a sister in Israel. And I had a brother in Shanghai, who came back after 10 years being in Shanghai, after the war. As a matter of fact, that was very funny. One time he, he wrote to me that he is going to be stationed when they went back from Shanghai to Israel. They went to Israel-- that he would be stationed and they will stop in France, in Marseilles. And, and then it changed. And they went to Italy, they went over Italy, you know, to Naples, Naples.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so I said, you know what? I had already at one child. I had Eileen. She was maybe by-- Eileen and I had-- yeah, I had Eileen only.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I took out a visa. And I went to see him, after 10 years not seeing him. And he was on the ship, the ship was standing there on the, on the-- on the pier.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I went up there, and he was peeling an orange. And I was behind him, and I said, can I have a piece of orange? And he turned around and said,

speaker: interviewee
text: [GASPS] The whole ship was coming and was screaming. And you know, I had-- they wouldn't believe that I would do that. So I saw my brother for the first time, my sister-in-law, and her parents were there. So that was that.

speaker: interviewer
text: But then this guy who was like-- the police guy who guarded the ship-- he wanted-- the Italian people, the men, are terrible. When they see a woman also they get crazy over it. And I was young, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said I should have a date with him. And I said, no, I can't do that. And then he didn't let me go on the ship anymore. And then I had to leave back to France.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you found all your siblings after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I found all my siblings after the war. They were all alive. We were all in different countries. The two were in America. One was in Shanghai. My sister and my brother were in Israel. And me, I was the only one who went to Hell. But that's fine, that's fine. I was praying in Auschwitz in the, in the train. I said, thank God. I am going myself. I, I was, I really didn't feel bad about it. I just wanted my, my brothers and sisters should stay alive. But it didn't happen that way, so thank God.

speaker: interviewer
text: So finally when was it, what year, when you came to America.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1953 we came to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was that trip like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The trip to America was eight day on the ship. But it was fine, because I knew my brother waited in New York for me. My youngest brother, Julius, he came from the army already. He was back from the army. And he came and, and, and took us to, to Chicago.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were in the United States Army during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm. My brother worked-- he was in the army, too. He was in in the-- the intelligence thing, department, there. My brother, Julius. I have the picture there from him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you live when you first came?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we first-- here in, here in America? We lived in, in East Rogers Park-- in East Rogers Park. As a matter of fact, we, we lived for the first maybe about two months or so, in a hotel on the sixth floor. And because I didn't want to go with my three children into anybody's house. We were very independent the whole time. We never went to a DP camp. We never took a penny from, from any of these organizations that offered. And we never-- we did it on our own, all the time. And when we were there for two months, we paid the rent in the hotel.

speaker: interviewer
text: I went into-- with all my three kids-- I went into a bakery. And a woman was there, and she said to me, where do you live? So I said, I live here in the-- in the hotel. So she said, you know what? I have an apartment for you, come to my house.

speaker: interviewee
text: I always was lucky they always liked, you know, to help me. So I said, OK. And we moved in there. And we paid $100 at the time rent for a month. And my husband started to work by [INAUDIBLE], you know. And he made about $55 a, a week, I guess. And so we started. And then, thank God, it, it went OK. We had hardships, but it was OK. And then later on, everything else changed. And my kids were going to day schools. I didn't-- we didn't go on vacation, but we saved the money for education. And that's what they got. If you-- education, a good Jewish upbringing in a good Jewish home. They went to day schools.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were you doing the years they grew up?

speaker: interviewee
text: When my kids-- I wanted to be with them, so I did a lot of volunteer work. I worked for the PTA. And I was a judge of election. And I worked for our-- my, for my synagogue. I did my share in volunteering then. As long as my husband made, made a living, it was fine. I didn't want riches anymore, and I didn't want luxuries. We didn't-- like I said, we didn't go on vacations. But the kids came first. And my kids say that they did not go into drugs or to other things, because they wanted to save us. Because they knew what we went through.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you start to tell your story?

speaker: interviewee
text: This story? After, after the Schindler book came out. To other people you-- I mean. My kids knew, my kids knew. But I did not want to publicize. Unless people asked me, I never talked about it. Till, till the movie-- till actually the book was written. And one day, Sunday, we have breakfast always in the-- I mean the shul has like lectures and breakfast. And my Rabi said, there's a book written. And we are going to discuss it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, what is the book? So he said, it's So I said, We were on there. So of course, you know, they wanted to ask us question. And that Sunday was like the Sunday for And we had to tell a little bit about it, but not very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, of course, the movie came out. And then we were asked-- my daughter worked for WBBA radio. Somehow they heard about it. They interviewed us on the radio. And then the TV stations came. And then the United Jewish Fund wanted to bring us out to all of these functions. So that's the way it started with the-- and then now the high schools and everybody else. And then we met-- we were invited to Governor Edgar's thing, when he said that everybody should have the movie, and they should read the book, and they should be educated about the Holocaust. And then Facing History came. And they wanted us, so we were the tutor-- the teachers are going to be teaching the children. So they wanted to know about us, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're very busy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, very busy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is-- what's your hope that will come from telling your story?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you what the hope is. My hope is what my mother said. That when-- if we, if I live through, that somebody else should know and be educated about what could happen, if you don't watch about bigotry, about race hating, and about all these things. Because everybody's born as a human being. And we all are human beings. We go and we, we are born and die like human beings. And that's the way it should be.

speaker: interviewer
text: I love everybody. You see, they ask me all the time, don't you hate-- one guy who interviewed me, he said, don't you hate the Germans? So I said, I tell you, it will not do me any good if I hate. I cannot forget, and I cannot forgive.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the hate will only eat me up. And life is too short to hate. Because you make yourself sick. If you hate somebody, you want to do something very bad to-- God is-- there is a God. I believe there is a God. Because every time I was saved, I said, it couldn't be. It's like an angel really, I don't see-- it's an angel. But it's God's will-- doing. I don't know why he did let six million die, and I was alive. [SOBBING] But that's another story. I cannot change that. I was guilty the whole time before I had children. I, I didn't go with my parents. But then when my children came, I said, maybe it's better I wasn't going with my parents. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it's pleasure when you see the children. And today too, the children are very, very important to me and my grandchildren. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's what I live for.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a philosophy in the camp that kept you going?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I had a philosophy my mother gave me. As long as you are alive and you, you are a person, you have hope. And, and, and maybe, maybe, you know, it's OK that you suffer. And then afterwards, it's worth the suffering. My mother was a very modern, wonderful woman. She was sewing all my clothes. And she was doing everything she could. One day I went to a ball-- to a you know, [INAUDIBLE] from the spot like we had in the evening. The morning before, she made me a fresh blouse. I should look nice in the evening. She was wonderful. All six children, we adored her. [SOBBING] But she didn't have anything. She got letters from my brothers, you know, in Poland. And she was going around to the family, and she was reading the letters. And she was reading it over and over again. Nobody was married when they were alive. We were all married afterwards. [SOBBING] But she didn't deserve, and that's the way it is. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else that you would like to add to this record?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just want to say that God should only be with all of the people to, to prevent something like this should ever happen again. Because there is no place in the world for bigotry and for race hating or something like that. Like I say, we are all born the same way and go the same way. And God is our justice. Yeah, [? Gret-- ?] I forgot to tell you about [? Gret. ?] After the war in Krakau, he was like in a trial. And we went to the trial. And he came from there-- they were-- he was taken from the prison into the court room. And he looked pathetic. He looked terrible. And so we were all standing-- I was going up. And there was like a, like a window sill or something. I was going up there-- on the street, like there was something. And I was clapping, everybody was clapping, to see him so pathetic looking. He was an [INAUDIBLE]. His secretary had all the documents and everything like a diary. And he was like talking to him and telling him that all. And so what happened was he was hanged but not in public. They didn't want to demoralize the people. So he was hanged, and we knew that he was gone, thank God. He was called an [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That-- this was a very good thing for me to know. It gave me a lot of satisfaction. He had two dogs. And he was just a monster. He wasn't a human being, [? Gret. ?] [? Gret ?] was a monster. You were afraid to look into his face. So was I afraid to look into Mengele's face. They both had faces--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I didn't even tell you about an incident in the ghetto. You want me to tell you about that? Please.

speaker: interviewee
text: What the ghetto was? Yeah. So the Polish people, we worked outside from the ghetto. They came in with a little bit of butter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told you-- and this was in Tarnow, still, in Tarnow. This was the ghetto in Tarnow. And we were like going in back to the camp, to the ghetto. And we came into the ghetto, and [? Grunow ?] was a Gestapo. He came there, we were maybe about 2,000 people in, in the Ghetto. Or maybe even more, I don't even know the numbers there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we came in, and a woman next to me had a pound of butter she bought. And when he was standing there, he wanted to find some food bought outside from the ghetto. He would-- you would be killed for that. So he wanted to find somebody.

speaker: interviewee
text: This woman had a pound of butter. And she told over the fence-- we were standing in the ghetto, next to the fence. And he saw that. And he thought it's me, because she was standing next to me. We both had like rain coats, long fur coats, with the hood on. He took my-- me by the hood like this. And he had already the gun on me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then he realized it was the next woman, which was the same way. I mean, you know, what difference does it make? So he let me go. And he took her instead and gave her one shot into the forehead here. She fell against the fence and was dead. So that was the life in the ghetto. You can imagine, every time we came in with something, we were afraid that we might be caught and, and shot. Everything was under that.

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived there in one kitchen and in one room. And there were two rooms in our apartment in the ghetto. And we lived there maybe about eight, nine people, just you know, we had to sleep in the kitchen and eat in the kitchen. That was-- this was where we were taken out to Plaszow, where my husband and the brother of my husband. This was the ghetto. And then before, that was my house to live. We had a lot of people. As a matter of fact, my little niece, who was given to a, to a farmer-- my husband paid for that. And she was brought back. And she was full of lice. She came back into the ghetto, and I had to wash her head. And she was screaming. It was burning, I had to put something on her head. It was just awful. And she was, anyhow, going to the gas chamber afterwards, so-- I saved-- we saved her, and it didn't help any. Such a sweetheart. My husband has-- I believe he has a picture. She looked like a Shirley Temple doll. But they didn't-- the Germans didn't care. They, they, they killed everybody the way they wanted to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your, your own children were how old when you started to tell your story to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I really cannot tell you exactly the age. But when they-- when they were of school age already by-- maybe they were eight, nine years old. I, I didn't tell them before, because what good will it be? They wouldn't understand. I don't believe that anybody who wasn't there can understand. I don't know whether you can understand the real conditions and everything. Nobody can understand. It's impossible, I mean. That's why we were-- last week we spoke in a church. And I told about Mengele. They didn't know who Mengele was. And I didn't explain it, because I thought everybody knows who Mengele was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So afterwards, the lady who took us there, she said, you know-- she knew a lot, I mean, she was educated in that regard. So she said, you know, you should have explained that a little bit better. They don't know who Mengele is. Is there anything else that you want to add for a message for people who see this tape? Young people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I, I would tell them that they should watch their government very much and the people who they vote for. Because it's very important. If the government-- it's what I always say. If the government is with you, if they're not against you-- like the Germans were against the Jews-- then it cannot really happen again. But if the government is watching, and if the politicians are there to watch too. It's very important who you vote for. You should know your people. You should be educated about that, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. If you want to ask anything else--

speaker: interviewer
text: And I'm beginning. You can begin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this is the Schindler List, made up in Krakau in the camp. And these are the women only, 300 women on the list. And there's my name, Schlesinger, Hania, which was a mistake with the first name. Because there was one lady I met in Auschwitz, she was my cousin, and I wanted her to go to Brunnlitz too. So they put my first name on here. This is Maria Weiner, it was supposed to be Hania Weiner. And that's the list of the 300 women who went to-- on Schindler's list. This is the Schindler's List for the, for the-- for the female prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us how you got this list, Margot.

speaker: interviewee
text: The list I got-- my, my granddaughter was in Israel [NON-ENGLISH]. And she went into Yad Vashem. And she said, my grandmother has to be on this list. And they gave her a copy of it. That's the way she brought it back to me. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And this is, this is Weiner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I see your name again, please. Yeah, sure. Here. It's alphabetically-- the, the men list is not alphabetically. So I am number, number 233 on the list. [INAUDIBLE]. And we're ready.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. These are papers which we got in Brunnlitz after the war, right after the war. It is dated May, May, 1945. And these were the only identification paper we had after the war. I was in Israel, of course. We had to give up, and we, we had nothing else. So at least we had something to identify us with. And we went to, to Tarnow after the war. And then in Tarnow we got another paper.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know what it says on this paper?

speaker: interviewee
text: On this-- this paper says that we are not political prisoners, and we have no sicknesses which are veneric, which-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and that we are normal people. We are not-- we are not criminals. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was to identify us.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, cut.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is from 1945, too. The 31st of October, 1945 they gave us another piece of paper. In Tarnow, we had to have something to identify us by, too. And this says only-- you know, like in passports, where we are from and who we are. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you go along the left side.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left side?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us what that says.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, on the left side it just says my name and my maiden name, my first name, my birth date, and where I was born, in Berlin. And that I have Polish citizen paper. And that I have no-- how do you say that, no, no marks to identify with me-- me by, no, no special marks. Which I actually have, but they never asked me for it. Yeah, I have here, on my-- a birthmark. These are my parents. And these pictures were taken in Germany in 1936 or so. My father did not-- he was a religious Jew, he really did not want to be photographed. But somehow, we got the photo-- we got him photographed. And my brother after the war gave it to me, gave me the picture, because I did not have anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] OK, tell us about this picture, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, this picture was taken 1937, where we knew that we will be separated, everybody of us. And we wanted to have a last picture. My brother Willie was already in Palestine. He left in 1936. And my sister was on [NON-ENGLISH], which meant she was prepared to go to Palestine, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we took that picture with my mom. My father didn't want to be photographed. Like I said, my father was religious, and he didn't believe in pictures taking. So my mother and my oldest brother is on the right sight. That's my oldest brother. My youngest brother is in the middle. And then on the left is my brother, Hy, who is actually the second brother. And then it's me and my mom in the-- sitting in the middle.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture I got from-- after the war, from my brother, Hy.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, tell us about this.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, my brother Julius-- my brother Julius, who was the youngest of the four brothers. He was in the army-- in the American army. And when he was finished, he went in 1946 to visit my sister, who was from [NON-ENGLISH]. She went from [NON-ENGLISH] to Israel. And my brother Willie, who was there from 1936, he went to visit them. And there this picture was taken. So this was the picture given to me by my other brother, who got it from them. My brother Hy got it from them, and he gave it to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are we clear who's on the life and who's on the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, on the right, on the right is my brother, Willie. In the middle is my sister, Shoshana. And my brother Julius is on the left.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's not rolling yet.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's not rolling. You can tell me. It's 45? Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken right after the war in 1945. And it was just before I got pregnant.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was at that time in-- we were in Tarnow, in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is that with you in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my husband is on the right. And me, I am, I am on the left side. That was taken in Tarnow in 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: Begin.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in 1947. And it was taken in Lyon, France, France, where my oldest daughter was born. And she was so tiny. But thank God, we had already a little bit bigger to-- to be-- a picture could have been taken. She was nine months old here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your daughter's--

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter's name is Eileen, Eileen. And now her married name is Eileen Yolcut. It was taken in 1947, in Lyon, France. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And placement in the picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anytime.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. OK, this picture was taken in 1953. I was visiting my brother, Julius-- my brother Willie and my brother Hy in Israel. And I-- on the right, on the right is my sister Shoshana. The next one sitting is my niece, Shulamit. And then the next one is me, Margot Schlesinger. And the next one is my sister, Rita Wind. And the next one is my sister, Pnina Wind. And the next row, there is my brother Willie on the right. In the middle is my brother-in-law, the husband of my sister Shoshana, Zvi is his name. And on the left is my brother Hy [NON-ENGLISH]. He just passed away three years ago, my brother Hy, who is the husband of my sister, Lorita.
